([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Henschel und Sohn*) was a German company, located in [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), best known during the 20th century as a maker of transportation equipment, including [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive"), [trucks](/wiki/Truck "Truck"), [buses](/wiki/Bus "Bus") and [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus"), and [armoured fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle") and [weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Museu_da_Tecnologia_-_Locomotiva_a_vapor_Henschel_%26_Sohn.JPG/330px-Museu_da_Tecnologia_-_Locomotiva_a_vapor_Henschel_%26_Sohn.JPG)](/wiki/File:Museu_da_Tecnologia_-_Locomotiva_a_vapor_Henschel_%26_Sohn.JPG)

Steam locomotive built by Henschel & Son in 1936, at the São Paulo Technology Museum, in Brazil

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Henschel_%26_Sohn_1920.JPG/250px-Henschel_%26_Sohn_1920.JPG)](/wiki/File:Henschel_%26_Sohn_1920.JPG)

Bond of Henschel & Sohn, issued February 1920

**Henschel & Son** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Henschel und Sohn*) was a German company, located in [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), best known during the 20th century as a maker of transportation equipment, including [locomotives](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive"), [trucks](/wiki/Truck "Truck"), [buses](/wiki/Bus "Bus") and [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus"), and [armoured fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle") and [weapons](/wiki/Weapon "Weapon").

Georg Christian Carl Henschel founded the factory in 1810 at Kassel. His son Carl Anton Henschel founded another factory in 1837. In 1848, the company began manufacturing locomotives. The factory became the largest locomotive manufacturer in Germany by the 20th century. Henschel built 10 articulated steam trucks, using [Doble steam designs](/wiki/Doble_steam_car "Doble steam car"), for [Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft "Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft") railways as delivery trucks. Several cars were built as well, one of which became [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring")'s staff car. In 1935 Henschel was able to upgrade its various steam locomotives to a high-speed [Streamliner](/wiki/Streamliner "Streamliner") type with a maximum speeds of up to 140 km/h (87 mph) by the addition of a removable shell over the old steam locomotive.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In 1918, Henschel began the production of gearboxes at the Kassel plant. In January 1925, Henschel & Son began building trucks and buses.

## World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_%26_Son&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Pr2_1800_%282%E2%80%99C2%E2%80%99_h2t%29.jpg/250px-Pr2_1800_%282%E2%80%99C2%E2%80%99_h2t%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pr2_1800_(2%E2%80%99C2%E2%80%99_h2t).jpg)

Henschel built (1941) 4-6-4 VR Class Pr2 steam locomotive (no. 1800) at [Haapamäki](/wiki/Haapam%C3%A4ki "Haapamäki") Steam Locomotive Museum in [Keuruu](/wiki/Keuruu "Keuruu"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1972-064-61%2C_Kassel%2C_Verladen_eines_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1972-064-61%2C_Kassel%2C_Verladen_eines_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1972-064-61,_Kassel,_Verladen_eines_Panzer_VI_%22Tiger_I%22.jpg)

A Tiger I is loaded onto a special rail car at the Henschel plant.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Henschel_Hs_129B.jpg/250px-Henschel_Hs_129B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henschel_Hs_129B.jpg)

The Henschel Hs 129B ground attack aircraft

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/German_locomotive_plate%2C_German_Museum_of_Technology%2C_Berlin.jpg/250px-German_locomotive_plate%2C_German_Museum_of_Technology%2C_Berlin.jpg)](/wiki/File:German_locomotive_plate,_German_Museum_of_Technology,_Berlin.jpg)

Locomotive [nameplate](/wiki/Builder%27s_plate "Builder's plate") (1883)

Early in 1935, Henschel began manufacturing [Panzer I](/wiki/Panzer_I "Panzer I") [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank"). During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the firm was responsible for license production of the [Dornier Do 17](/wiki/Dornier_Do_17 "Dornier Do 17")Z medium bomber, and in 1939–1940 it began large-scale production of the [Panzer III](/wiki/Panzer_III "Panzer III"). Henschel was the sole manufacturer of the [Tiger I](/wiki/Tiger_I "Tiger I"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and alongside [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") the [Tiger II](/wiki/Tiger_II "Tiger II"). In 1945, the company had 8,000 workers working in two shifts each of 12 hours, and [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") was used extensively. The company's factories, which also manufactured [narrow-gauge](/wiki/Narrow-gauge "Narrow-gauge") locomotives, were among the most important Allied bomber targets and were nearly completely destroyed.

### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_%26_Son&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Aviation")]

Henschel Flugzeugwerke aircraft and missiles included:

* [Henschel Hs 117](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_117 "Henschel Hs 117") *Schmetterling* (Butterfly), [rocket](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket")-engined [surface-to-air missile](/wiki/Surface-to-air_missile "Surface-to-air missile")
* [Henschel Hs 121](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_121 "Henschel Hs 121"), fighter and trainer (prototype)
* [Henschel Hs 122](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_122 "Henschel Hs 122"), [army co-operation](/wiki/Army_cooperation_aircraft "Army cooperation aircraft")/reconnaissance
* [Henschel Hs 123](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_123 "Henschel Hs 123"), ground-attack (biplane)
* [Henschel Hs 124](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_124 "Henschel Hs 124"), heavy fighter and bomber (prototype)
* [Henschel Hs 125](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_125 "Henschel Hs 125"), fighter and trainer (prototype)
* [Henschel Hs 126](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_126 "Henschel Hs 126"), reconnaissance
* [Henschel Hs 127](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_127 "Henschel Hs 127"), fast medium bomber (*Schnellbomber* prototype)
* [Henschel Hs 128](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_128 "Henschel Hs 128"), high-altitude reconnaissance and bomber (prototypes)
* [Henschel Hs 129](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_129 "Henschel Hs 129"), ground-attack
* [Henschel Hs 130](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_130 "Henschel Hs 130"), high altitude reconnaissance and bomber (prototypes)
* [Henschel Hs 132](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_132 "Henschel Hs 132"), [jet-engined](/wiki/Jet-engine "Jet-engine") dive bomber (prototype)
* [Henschel Hs 135](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_Hs_135&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henschel Hs 135 (page does not exist)"), [delta wing](/wiki/Delta_wing "Delta wing")
* [Henschel Hs 293](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_293 "Henschel Hs 293"), [rocket](/wiki/Walter_HWK_109-507 "Walter HWK 109-507")-boosted [glide bomb](/wiki/Glide_bomb "Glide bomb")
* [Henschel Hs 294](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_294 "Henschel Hs 294"), rocket-powered anti-shipping glide bomb
* [Henschel Hs 295](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_Hs_295&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henschel Hs 295 (page does not exist)")
* [Henschel Hs 296](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_Hs_296&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henschel Hs 296 (page does not exist)")
* [Henschel Hs 297](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_297 "Henschel Hs 297") *Föhn*, 73mm anti-aircraft rocket-launcher
* [Henschel Hs 298](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_298 "Henschel Hs 298"), rocket-powered [air-to-air missile](/wiki/Air-to-air_missile "Air-to-air missile")
* [Henschel Projekt P.75](/wiki/Henschel_Projekt_P.75 "Henschel Projekt P.75"), a 1941 design with slightly swept-back wings placed at the rear, swept-back [canards](/wiki/Canard_(aeronautics) "Canard (aeronautics)") at the front, and double [pusher propellers](/wiki/Pusher_configuration "Pusher configuration") at the rear.
* [Henschel Projekt P.87](/wiki/Henschel_Projekt_P.87 "Henschel Projekt P.87"), a [Schnellbomber](/wiki/Schnellbomber "Schnellbomber") design similar to the Hs P.75, except that the canards in the front are straight and the wing is curved.
* [Henschel 'Zitterrochen'](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_%27Zitterrochen%27&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henschel 'Zitterrochen' (page does not exist)") (Torpedofish), [air-to-surface missile](/wiki/Air-to-surface_missile "Air-to-surface missile") (pre-production only)

## Trolleybuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_%26_Son&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Trolleybuses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Restored_Solingen_Henschel-Uerdingen_trolleybus_59_with_doors_open%2C_at_depot_%282023%29.jpg/250px-Restored_Solingen_Henschel-Uerdingen_trolleybus_59_with_doors_open%2C_at_depot_%282023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Restored_Solingen_Henschel-Uerdingen_trolleybus_59_with_doors_open,_at_depot_(2023).jpg)

A preserved Henschel [trolleybus](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") in [Solingen](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_Solingen "Trolleybuses in Solingen") (with Henschel nameplate and logo on front)

Manufacturing of trolleybuses began in 1941 and continued until 1962, ultimately totalling at least 680 vehicles,[[5]](#cite_note-murray-5) while Henschel also constructed the chassis for more than 240 others that used bodies by [Waggonfabrik Uerdingen](/wiki/Waggonfabrik_Uerdingen "Waggonfabrik Uerdingen") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waggonfabrik_Uerdingen "de:Waggonfabrik Uerdingen")] (which changed its name to [Duewag](/wiki/Duewag "Duewag") many years later), of [type ÜHIIIs](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%9CHIIIs "de:ÜHIIIs") and [ÜHIIs](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%9CHIIs "de:ÜHIIs"). Almost all were purchased by transport companies in Germany or Austria, but Henschel's single largest order for trolleybuses was from [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires"), Argentina, for 175 vehicles built in 1952–1953, and the [São Paulo trolleybus system](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Trolleybuses in São Paulo") purchased 50 Henschel–Uerdingen trolleybuses in 1954.[[5]](#cite_note-murray-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Moraes-Howe-2022-6): 107–108 All but 50 of the 175 Buenos Aires vehicles were fitted with bodies made by Nordwestdeutscher Fahrzeugbau.[[7]](#cite_note-morrison-tm198-7): 150

## Post-war business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Henschel_%26_Son&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Post-war business")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Henschel_%26_Son_1951_engine.JPG/250px-Henschel_%26_Son_1951_engine.JPG)](/wiki/File:Henschel_%26_Son_1951_engine.JPG)

1951 restored pony engine

Manufacturing began again in 1948. In 1964, the company took over Rheinische Stahlwerke and became Rheinstahl Henschel AG ([Hanomag](/wiki/Hanomag "Hanomag")). The truck production of Henschel was merged with that of Hanomag that spun off to form Hanomag-Henschel in 1969, this later went to Daimler-Benz, which discontinued the brand name Hanomag-Henschel in 1974. The production was switched to commercial vehicle axles, in this area it is the largest factory in Europe. In 1976 [Thyssen-Henschel](/wiki/Thyssen-Henschel "Thyssen-Henschel"), and 1990 ABB Henschel AG. In 1996, the company became [ABB Daimler Benz Transportation Adtranz](/wiki/Adtranz "Adtranz"). The company was subsequently acquired by [Bombardier](/wiki/Bombardier_Inc. "Bombardier Inc.") (Canada) around 2002. In 2021 [Alstom](/wiki/Alstom "Alstom") acquired [Bombardier Transportation](/wiki/Bombardier_Transportation "Bombardier Transportation"). The Kassel facility still exists and is one of the world's largest manufacturers of locomotives.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Main product built and serviced today in Kassel is the [Alstom Traxx](/wiki/Alstom_Traxx "Alstom Traxx").
